address.addressline: Department of Rare Books and Manuscripts

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Yale Center for British Art, Rare Books and Manuscripts

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday November 19, 2019 at 22:01

corpname.part: Yale Center for British Art, Rare Books and Manuscripts

bibliography.head: Bibliography
bibliography.bibref: The Independent Group: postwar Britain and the aesthetics of plenty. Edited by David Robbins. Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, c1990
McHale, John. The expendable ikon. Buffalo, NY: Albright-Knox Art Gallery, 1984

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Much of McHale's works of the 1950s are devoted to (re)processing media information. There is a recurrent interest in the processing of information through communication systems. (See the lecture material) His collage books are best understood as efforts to enact/create such systems in a viewer-interactive form.

did.unittitle: Why I Took to the Washers in Luxury Flats

odd.head: General
odd.p: Signed on front cover: John McHale '54

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Use requires permission of the Curator of Rare Books and Manuscripts.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "McHale at this time abandoned the neural forms of his earlier work in order to emphasize the role of communications media. He wished to visualize how raw data (represented by torn bits of colored paper, newspapers, and magazines) might be organized by a human or mechanical brain into discrete messages. The collage books that he produced in 1954 (cat. no. 37) synthesized the themes of mass culture, information processing, and viewer participation. Each page of these books (one of which was shown in the ICA's Collages and Objects exhibition in 1954) is composed of a collage cut into strips, so that the volume becomes a bound series of variable collages to be 'read' however the viewer wishes."--Baas, Jacquelynn. "John McHale" In The Independent Group: postwar Britain and the aesthetics of plenty. Edited by David Robbins. Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, c1990.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Use requires permission of the Curator of Rare Books and Manuscripts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Shoe-Life Stories (circa 1954) sliced reproductions of faces vertically and alternated these with textures of objects from a jeweler's catalog."--Buckley, Craig. Graphic Assembly: Montage, Media, and Experimental Architecture in the 1960. University of Minnesota Press, 2019, page 50.

did.physdesc: 1 collage book (unfinished) : magazine tear sheets cut in strips and glued to heavy-stock paper ; 25 x 42 cm
1 collage book (unfinished) : magazine tear sheets cut in strips and glued to heavy-stock paper ; 30 x 46 cm
1 collage : brown kraft paper, gray construction paper and yellow and black coated papers ; 19.5 x 59 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From a series of works called Transistors. These works serve as visual analogs to the processing of information. The invention of the transistor in 1948 led to the development of the electronic computer, as well as smaller radios and other communication devices. McHale's works relate to such new technological phenomena.

did.physdesc: 1 collage : black ink on white paper and aluminum foil ; 47.5 x 39.5 cm, in framed 65 x 56.5 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Aluminum was a lightweight construction material increasingly used in the 1950s by architects. It is sometimes associated with the New Pragmaticist architecture being built in Britain in these years. McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 collage : orange yellow and white construction paper with black fiber-tip pen on white wove paper ; 60.5 x 46 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 collage : magazine and newspaper tear sheets and black construction paper on wove paper ; 56.5 x 46 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 collage : magazine tear sheets on green construction paper, mounted on purple paper ; 65 x 47.5

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 97 items : magazine and newspaper tear sheets ; 26 x 10 - 27 x 36 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Periodicals used include Life, London Sunday Times, Look and Saturday Evening Post; included "A 100 mile high portrait of Earth" (from Look) and an almost complete copy of Life, April 11, 1955 issue; 1 advertisement in this issue is noted as being used in Richard Hamilton's A-A-AH.

did.physdesc: 106 items : newspaper and magazine clippings, tear sheets, articles and a business card ; 10 x 15 - 27 x 36 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Periodicals used include Madison Avenue, New Yorker, and Time; one of the photographs depicts McHale's Virginia Imported, 1957.

did.physdesc: 7 drawings : graphite on paper ; 30 x 24 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: associated with palette #2

did.physdesc: collage pieces from tear sheets and paper; two photographs, one pen on paper drawing (folded) - 7 items

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: associated with palette #2; the photographs are likely, but not necessarily related to the collage book project; the drawing illustrated a plan for the book format.

did.physdesc: magazine and newspaper tear sheets, construction paper - 127 items

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: last working palette although a number of tear sheets are dated earlier (1950s); some are prepared for collage

did.physdesc: 3 items : magazine and newspaper tear sheets, construction paper - 3 items

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Partly assembled collage parts, pieces of construction paper, oversized palette material. Includes a portion of the Houston Chronicle, section 1, for September 1, 1977.

did.physdesc: four magazine and newspaper tear sheets, photograph

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: tearsheet from tack board in John McHale's London studio c.1956 as depicted in the included Sam Lambert photograph which depicts John McHale and Magda Cordell in the studio with the tackboard in the background.

did.physdesc: 1 collage : paper collage mounted on masonite board ; 121.6 x 110.2 cm
1 painting : oil and paper collage on canvas ; 152.4 x 101.6 cm
1 painting : oil and paper collage on canvas ; 152.1 x 101.3 cm
1 drawing : brush and black ink (possibly on japan paper) ; 56.5 x 44.5 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One of three in a series of geometric drawings, 1952.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : brush and black ink on wove paper with deckled edges ; 60 x 43 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One of three in a series of geometric drawings, 1952.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One of three in a series of geometric drawings, 1952.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : pen and black ink with gray wash ; 53.3 x 38

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Aluminum was a lightweight construction material increasingly used in the 1950s by architects. It is sometimes associated with the New Pragmaticist architecture being built in Britain in these years. McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : pen and brush, black ink and gray wash ; sheet 91 x 58.5 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Aluminum was a lightweight construction material increasingly used in the 1950s by architects. It is sometimes associated with the New Pragmaticist architecture being built in Britain in these years. McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : pen and black ink (possibly on japan paper) ; 28 x 21 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Aluminum was a lightweight construction material increasingly used in the 1950s by architects. It is sometimes associated with the New Pragmaticist architecture being built in Britain in these years. McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : brush and black and red paint on shiny wove paper ; sheet 91.5 x 59.5 cm
1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen on shiny tearsheet from yearbook (photos on verso) ; 54 x 38 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: from a series of large scale drawings using various pre-columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : brush and black poster paint and black fiber-tip pen on shiny tearsheet from yearbook (photos on verso) ; sheet 76 x 54 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: from a series of large scale drawings using various pre-columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen with red shiny construction paper on shiny tearsheet from yearbook (photos on verso) ; sheet 77 x 54 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: from a series of large scale drawings using various pre-columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen on shiny tearsheet from yearbook (photos on verso) ; sheet 78 x 54 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: from a series of large scale drawings using various pre-columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : brown fiber-tip pen on wove paper ; sheet 97 x 64 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: from a series of large scale drawings using various pre-columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : red and black fiber-tip pen on wove paper ; sheet 94 x 63.5 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: from a series of large scale drawings using various pre-columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen on wove paper ; sheet 94 x 63.5 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: from a series of large scale drawings using various pre-columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: one sketchpad, with disbound leaves and 24 additional laid-in drawings; includes pen and ink and pencil drawings; four photocopies of ball point pen drawings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The majority of these drawings are probably preliminary sketches to the larger fiber-tip pen/poster paint drawings in the collection, although a few drawings from this collection of sketches seem to be unrelated. The ball point pen drawings in this series are revisions of earlier pencil sketches. There is also a drawing [B1995.24.12(39)] which is derived from a magazine tear-sheet in Palette#2; in the subsequent sketch [B1995.24.12(4)] the design is further abstracted and reintegrated back into a 'Head' composed of Pre-Columbian motifs.

did.unittitle: Sheets prepared by McHale for discussion of lecture by E.W. 'Bingo' Meyers, entitled "Probability and Information Theory and their Application to the Visual Arts" given March 8, 1955 to the Independent Group at the ICA

did.physdesc: 1 diagram : black and red fiber-tip pen with black construction paper ; sheet 58 x 91 cm
1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen ; sheet 91 x 58 cm
1 drawing : black and red fiber-tip pen with blue-gray construction paper and magazine tear-sheets ; sheet 58 x 91 cm
1 graph : black, yellow, and blue fiber-tip pen and graphite ; sheet 91 x 58 cm
1 table : black, red, and green fiber-tip pen and blue-gray construction paper ; 91 x 58 cm

did.unittitle: Frequency of Occurrence of Letters in English in 1000 Letters
did.physdesc: 1 chart : black and red fiber-tip pen with graphite ; sheet 58 x 91 cm
1 drawing (?) : black fiber-tip pen with yellow and black construction paper ; sheet 91 x 58 cm

did.unittitle: Paintings Drawings, Sculptures by Jean Dubuffet

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: at the I.C.A., Institute of Contemporary Arts Gallery, 17-18 Dover Street, London W1; 30 March - 30 April

did.physdesc: 1 poster : screen print with red and black ink ; sheet 51 x 76 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The exhibition was divided into various collaborative 'groups' of architects and artists. Each of these groups produced its own poster for the exhibition. This poster was likely produced independently from the group posters.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: at the Whitechapel Art Gallery; August 9 - September 9 1956; poster designed by McHale

did.physdesc: 1 poster; screen print with black ink ; 77 x 51 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A screen print of Hamilton's Just what is it that makes today's homes so different, so appealing?; poster for Group Two. The exhibition was divided into various collaborative 'groups' of architects and artists. Each of these groups produced its own poster for the exhibition. McHale was in Group Two with Richard Hamilton and John Voelcker.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: at the Whitechapel Art Gallery; August 9 - September 9 1956; poster designed by Richard Hamilton

did.physdesc: exhibition poster; screen print with black ink

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A screen print of an altered photograph of Peter Smithson, Eduardo Paolozzi, Alison Smithson, and Nigel Henderson; photo of Group Six by Henderson. The exhibition was divided into various collaborative 'groups' of architects and artists. Each of these groups produced its own poster for the exhibition.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: at the Whitechapel Art Gallery; August 9 - September 9 1956; poster designed by Nigel Henderson

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: at the I.C.A., Institute of Contemporary Arts, 17-18 Dover Street, London W1; 6 September - 13 October 1962; credit: Gordon House and Kelpra Studio Ltd., London

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: After his 1967 article with the same title. McHale comments on the revolutionary impact of technology on contemporary culture. The article discusses the loss of visible discrete cultural products in favor of a "continuous contextual flow," the future of art as residing in the innovation of "communicative gestures in multi-media forms," the new interchangeability of art and non-art, and the move in the definition of art from the object's "intrinsic value" to its conception in relation to "life, style, and orientation."

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: lecture by John McHale; the Carpenter Center for the Visual Arts, Harvard University; December 12, 1968, 8:30pm; poster designed by Toshihiro Katayama

did.unittitle: Catalogs, periodicals, ephemera, and small posters
Institute of Contemporary Arts : sculpture exhibition arranged by Lawrence Alloway

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lists works by Robert Adams, Kenneth Armitage, Trevor Bates, Reg Butler, Lynn Chadwick, Geoffrey Clarke, Elizabeth Frink, Barbara Hepworth, John McHale, Bernard Meadows, Henry Moore, Eduardo Paolozzi, William Turnbull, and Rosemary Young.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photocopy of the original brochure. "Exhibition organized by Lawrence Alloway; 13 October - 20 November 1954; London: Institute of Contempoary Arts, 1954."--Leaf [2]. With a list of the 83 items exhibited, including two collages books by John McHale: Palimpcestuous (no. 81) and Secret Life of a Talisman (no. 82).

did.unittitle: The Independent Group, schedule of seminars for Spring 1955 session
did.physdesc: 2 sheets ([2] pages) : mimeograph typescript ; 33 x 21 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: John McHale is listed as a speaker in x of the seminars: Paintings by Richard Hamilton (February 11); Advertising, 1 (April 15); Dadaists as Non-Aristotelians (April 29); Advertising, 2 (May 27). Other seminar speakers include: Lawrence Alloway, Reyner Banham, Frank Cordell, Gillo Dorfles, Richard Hamilton, Anthony Hill, Donald Holmes, E.W. Meyer, Eduardo Paolozzi, Tony del Renzio, and Peter Smithson.

did.physdesc: 1 folded sheet : illustrations ; 16 x 37 cm, folded 16 x 13 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brochure. Subtitle: "27 November-15 December 1956. ICA Library, 17-18 Dover Street, London, W1." With an enumeration of the collages: "1. Zebra. 2. Puffhead. 3. Figurehead. 4. Rosehead. 5. Flexible head. 6. Chocolate head. 7. Dial. 8. Maphead. 9. Silverhead. 10. Furhead. 11. Olive head."

did.unittitle: John McHale and Magda Cordell in studio, with tack board bearing tear sheets from McHale's Palette #4
did.physdesc: 1 photograph : gelatin silver print ; 17 x 16 cm

odd.head: General
odd.p: The photograph is a reproduction, not the original.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Exhibition announcement, designed by Toni Del Renzio. "Arranged by Lawrence Alloway with the co-operation of the ICA at the O'Hana Gallery, 3 Carlos Place, Grosvenor Square W1, December 6 to 21 …"

did.unittitle: 3 Collagists: New work by E.L.T Mesens, John McHale, Gwyther Irwin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Small exhibition poster. At the Institute of Contemporary Arts, 5-29 November 1958.

did.physdesc: 1 folded sheet ; 26 x 26 cm, folded to 10 x 26 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes artists' statements by Irwin, McHale, and Mesens. At the Institute of Contemporary Arts, 1958.

did.unittitle: Class of '59 : Magda Cordell, Eduardo Paolozzi, John McHale : paintings, sculpture, collages

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: At The Union, Cambridge, February 7-19, 1959. Arranged by the Cambridge Contemporary Art Trust. One side of the sheet serves as a small poster. The verso includes a checklist and a commentary by Lawrence Alloway.

did.unittitle: Lady Clare : a review : volume XLIV, June 1959

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "The Human Image" by Robert Freeman, pages 6-9, with commentary on the work of Magda Cordell and John McHale; and "Images of the mass media" by John McHale, pages 10-11.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Cambridge: Amalgamated Clubs of Clare College, 1959

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Presented by the Blackfriars Settlement ... at the Institute of Contemporary Arts ... 27 April to 12 May 1962 … Louis le Brocquey, Anne Buchanan, John Ernest, Andrew Forge, John McHale, Sidney Nolan, John Plumb, William Scott, Peter Stroud, Joe Tilson, and William Turnbull." With text describing the Blackfriars Settlement.

did.physdesc: 1 folded sheet (3 panels) ; 21 x 60 cm, folded to 21 x 20 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brochure for an exhibition at the Institute of Contemporary Arts, 1962. Includes commentaries by R. Buckminster Fuller (on Magda Cordell's Presences) and Reyner Banham (on John McHale's Transition).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "The Plastic Parthenon" by John McHale, pages 4-11.
